references.name: Patagonia
references.takeaway: Use full-bleed landscape photographs as section backgrounds with text overlaid, and derive the entire UI color palette from the natural tones in those photographs (sand, sage, stone, sky).
howApplied: The entire UI palette is derived from natural desert landscape tones: #f0e6d6 (sand), #c4622a (terracotta/red rock), #7a8a6a (sage vegetation), #d4c0a0 (dry clay). Every color references a physical material found in the desert environment.

The Dashboard view uses landscape-scale visual storytelling: stat formations are presented as mesa landforms at different heights, giving the dashboard a topographic quality rather than a flat grid of numbers.

Activism/mission content (conservation, wildlife protection, trail stewardship) is given equal visual weight throughout — the Kanban tasks, Ideas, and Chat all foreground conservation work alongside operational tasks, mirroring Patagonia's editorial-operational blend.
specificElements: Palette colors #f0e6d6, #c4622a, #7a8a6a, #d4c0a0 all derived from sandstone canyon photography tones
Mesa-height stat formations in Dashboard view mimic landscape elevation profiles
Content persona centers conservation, wildlife observation, and trail stewardship themes throughout all views

references.name: Allbirds
references.takeaway: Give each content category its own warm earth-tone background (sandstone beige, mesa red, sage green, sky blue) and tell a material story for each — what it is, where it comes from, how it feels.
howApplied: Each Kanban lane has its own earth-tone identity via the canyon-layer depth system: the Basecamp lane uses terracotta top border (#c4622a), On Trail uses sage (#7a8a6a), Under Review uses amber (#c49a2a), and Summit Reached uses clay (#d4c0a0). This gives each category its own material identity.

The warm neutral background (#f0e6d6) throughout the application mirrors Allbirds' unbleached cotton/raw linen feeling. The surface cards (#f8f0e4) sit slightly lighter, creating depth through material warmth rather than stark contrast.

The Schema Planner view tells a 'material story' for each entity — entities are presented as rock formation samples with geological field types, and relationships are fault lines. This gives the data model a tangible, physical material quality.
specificElements: Canyon layer depth system on Kanban lanes — each lane has its own warm earth-tone border (terracotta, sage, amber, clay)
Background #f0e6d6 replicates the warm neutral feeling of natural unbleached materials
Rock formation entity blocks in Schema Planner tell the 'material story' of each data entity

references.name: National Park Foundation
references.takeaway: Use WPA-poster-inspired illustrated badges and trail-marker iconography as section headers and navigation waypoints, giving the layout the feel of a trail map through a sandstone canyon.
howApplied: The Projects view is structured as a literal trail map with SVG winding path and circular waypoint markers positioned along the route, directly referencing the park-finder map interface with terrain-based visual navigation.

The Settings view is designed as a trail guide wizard where each step is a numbered trail marker waypoint with 'terrain descriptions' explaining the content of each section, using the language and structure of trail guide publications.

Navigation toolbar buttons use small iconic SVGs and compact labeling inspired by park trail sign design — functional, identifiable at a glance, with terracotta active indicators that reference painted trail blazes.
specificElements: Projects view: winding SVG trail path with circle waypoint markers, elevation and distance metadata per project
Settings view: numbered waypoint markers (1-4) connected by trail connector lines, 'Terrain:' descriptions for each section
Toolbar navigation: compact icon + label format inspired by park wayfinding signage, terracotta active indicator bar as trail blaze
